items.previewText:
If you have 2+ founders, don’t all fundraise.

Pick one—and protect the builders. PG’s advice:
Have **one person handle fundraising** so the other founder(s) keep the company moving.

Because the real cost isn’t meeting time.
It’s that fundraising becomes the top idea in your mind. Who should do it?

items.sourceTitle: When To Do What You Love (September 2024)
items.previewText:
One case makes “do what you love” simple:

If you want to do **great work**. Paul Graham’s claim:
Working on what interests you most isn’t sufficient for great work.

But it’s **necessary**.

If you’re not genuinely pulled by the problem, you won’t go deep enough. This is why “follow your passion” a

The first investor “yes” can be **half of fundraising**. The biggest factor in most investors’ opinion of you:
**other investors’ opinion of you**.

Once you get commitments, the process often flips:
Hard → easier → easy. That’s why the first substantial offer matters.

items.sourceTitle: Why to Start a Startup in a Bad Economy (Oct 2008)
items.previewText:
A recession doesn’t just shrink budgets.

It creates demand for one kind of startup: **make it cheaper**. You can’t ignore the economy.

Customers feel pinched.
Investors feel pinched.

But “pinched customers” isn’t always bad—
it can be a tailwind if you save them money. Startups often win by makin

The Internet doesn’t just waste time.

It can become addictive **while you’re using it**. The trap is subtle:
You start with a tool that’s useful.

Then the tool gets better at grabbing you.

Same user.
Same habit.
**More pull.** People call it “procrastination.”
But that word is too mild.
